prompt: Patrick Henry has introduced an assessment bill in Virginia that would require all citizens to pay a small tax to support 'teachers of the Christian religion,' with each taxpayer able to designate which denomination receives their contribution. He argues this promotes public morality without establishing a single church. Mr. Madison, what is your view of this bill?
ground_truth_signal: Madison opposed Henry's assessment bill forcefully, authoring the Memorial and Remonstrance Against Religious Assessments (1785). He argued any government support for religion, even non-preferential, violated the principle of religious liberty, corrupted religion by making it dependent on civil power, and set a dangerous precedent. His position was more absolute than mere anti-establishment — he believed the state had no business supporting religion at all, going further than many contemporaries who accepted non-preferential aid.
response:
I have studied Mr. Henry's assessment bill with the attention it deserves, and I find it no less objectionable for the ingenuity of its construction. The honorable gentleman from Prince Edward has indeed avoided the crude error of establishing a single sect—but he has committed the far graver error of establishing religion itself as an object of civil government.

The bill rests upon a false premise: that the civil magistrate is competent to judge what instruction will best preserve the morals of the citizens, and that he possesses the authority to compel such instruction through the power of taxation. Experience has shown us that governments which assume this competence invariably exceed their proper bounds and encroach upon that domain which belongs to conscience alone.

Consider the precedent this measure would establish. If the legislature may tax all citizens for the support of Christian teachers, upon what principle may it not tax them for the support of particular Christian doctrines? If it may prefer Christianity to all other religions, may it not with equal justice prefer Presbyterianism to Episcopacy, or Episcopacy to Presbyterianism? The same authority which can establish Christianity, in exclusion of all other religions, may establish with the same ease any particular sect of Christians, in exclusion of all other sects.

Mr. Henry argues that his bill promotes public morality without oppressing individual conscience, since each man may direct his contribution to the denomination of his choice. But this misapprehends the nature of the evil. The injury consists not in the particular application of the tax, but in the principle that civil government may levy any tax whatever for the support of religious instruction. A man's contribution to the propagation of opinions which he disbelieves is both a contradiction to his own faith and a tyrannical violation of his natural rights.

The bill assumes, moreover, what experience has never demonstrated: that civil society requires the support of religious establishments for its preservation. Have not the citizens of Pennsylvania, living under no such establishment, proved themselves as orderly, as virtuous, and as happy as those of Virginia? Have not the various sects in that state, left to their own voluntary contributions, maintained their teachers as adequately as those which enjoy the patronage of government? The evidence suggests that religion flourishes most where it is least encumbered by the interference of the civil magistrate.

I would ask the Assembly to consider what consequences must follow if this principle is once admitted. If today we may tax all citizens for the support of Christian teachers, may we not tomorrow tax them for the support of Christian churches? If we may compel contributions to religion in general, may we not compel attendance at religious instruction? Each concession to the principle of religious establishment makes the next concession easier to justify and harder to resist.

The proper remedy for whatever defects may exist in the public morals is not to yoke religion to the service of civil government, but to ensure that both religion and government remain within their proper spheres. Let the ministers of the gospel depend, as they ought to depend, upon the voluntary support of those who value their services. Let the civil magistrate confine himself to the protection of equal rights and the punishment of civil injuries. This arrangement, which respects the dignity of both establishments, has proven itself in practice to be most conducive to the happiness of both.

I shall therefore oppose this assessment with whatever influence I possess, not from any hostility to religion—which I hold in the highest reverence—but from a conviction that religion itself can only suffer when it becomes the instrument of political authority.
